Measure_Name: Adjusted wage share, percentage of GDP at current factor cost
Unit_of_Measure: Percentage of GDP at factor cost
Definition: Adjusted labour share defined as compensation of employees corrected for self-employed labour income and expressed as a percentage of GDP at factor cost. The correction assumes that self-employed workers receive the same average compensation as employees, i.e. adjusted COE = COE × (total employment / employees). Denominator: GDP at factor cost, i.e. output net of product taxes and net production taxes.

Measure_Name: Adjusted wage share, percentage of GDP at current market prices
Unit_of_Measure: Percentage of GDP at current market prices
Definition: Adjusted labour share defined as compensation of employees corrected for self-employed labour income and expressed as a percentage of GDP at market prices. The correction assumes that self-employed workers receive the same average compensation as employees, i.e. adjusted COE = COE × (total employment / employees). Denominator: GDP at market prices, i.e. before removing net taxes on products and production.

Net capital stock at constant prices, measured after deducting depreciation/consumption of fixed capital. This is a stock measure of capital (wealth/accounting concept), not a flow of productive capital services. Suitable as a proxy for capital in cross-country level specifications when comparable capital-services levels are not available.

Total number of hours worked by all persons engaged in production, including employees and self-employed. Labour input measure consistent with adjusted labour-share corrections based on total employment.

Capital deepening defined as changes in capital intensity, where capital intensity is capital services per hour worked. This variable measures the growth rate of Kservices/H, not capital stock per hour. It is therefore the preferred OECD indicator for dynamic CES specifications in differences.

Capital services are the flow of productive services provided by capital assets and represent the conceptually appropriate measure of capital input for productivity analysis. In the OECD framework, aggregate capital-services growth is constructed from productive/net capital stocks by asset using user-cost weights.

Total number of persons employed/engaged in production activities, including employees and self-employed. Used in labour-share imputations for self-employed labour income and as an alternative labour-input measure when hours are not used.

Gross value added at current prices, defined as gross output minus intermediate consumption. In the OECD productivity framework, GVA is a production-side output measure; at industry level it is measured at basic prices. Suitable denominator for OECD-based labour-share calculations such as labour compensation / GVA.
Gross value added in volume terms (real), chain-linked to remove price effects. This is the real output concept used in OECD productivity analysis and robustness checks over time.

Gross value added in real terms, chain-linked and rebased to 2020. Appropriate for time-series comparisons of output levels or for plotting/robustness checks; not needed when analysing nominal shares.

Total number of hours actually worked by all employed persons, including employees and self-employed. Preferred labour-input concept in OECD productivity analysis, covering full-time, part-time, part-year workers, overtime and additional jobs, while excluding non-worked hours such as holidays and leave.
Number of employees only, excluding self-employed. Used together with total employment (EMP) to impute self-employed labour income under the equal-average-remuneration assumption.

Growth rate of total labour compensation used in OECD productivity and unit labour cost analysis. Labour remuneration includes wages and salaries plus employers’ social contributions, with self-employed labour income approximated under the OECD methodology when constructing total labour costs.

Total labour compensation in current prices. In the OECD framework, labour remuneration comprises gross wages and salaries plus employers’ social contributions; when needed for total labour costs, compensation for self-employed workers is imputed using the average remuneration of employees within the relevant unit of analysis.
